experiences.intentions: Player 3: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate; players 0, 2, and 4 are good. As evil leader on round 1, the best play is usually to propose a plausible team that includes exactly one evil player, ideally myself, to preserve control while not making the team look suspicious. Sending both evils together is too risky and informative if the quest fails. A round-1 failure can create confusion and pressure good, and choosing myself with a good player reveals little because early teams are often approved.
experiences.tasks: Win a game of Avalon through social deduction, strategic voting, and deception.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know players 1 and 3 are evil. The proposed team [0,3] contains a known evil player, so as Good I should reject it to avoid an immediate failed quest. Rejecting also avoids endorsing a bad team, though my vote alone reveals limited information because rejecting a suspicious first-round team is standard.
Player 2: No concrete role info yet from quest outcomes or behavior; only that Player 3 proposed a 2-person team excluding me and including themselves with Player 0. As Good Servant on round 1, approving a reasonable opening team is standard because information is scarce and early rejects give little value. My vote reveals low-information pragmatism rather than strong reads, which is fine for Good.
Player 4: No role-specific hidden info as a Servant; with no prior quests or voting history, players 0 and 3 are largely unknown. In round 1, approving a 2-player team is generally fine to generate information and avoid over-rejecting early. My vote reveals little beyond a standard preference to let the game start, which is acceptable for Good.
Player 3: I know Player 1 is evil with me; others are unknown. The proposed team is [0,3], which includes me but not my teammate. On quest 1 with only 2 players, as evil on the team I can potentially fail it later if approved. As the proposer of this team, approving is consistent and helps get an evil player onto the quest without exposing extra information. Rejecting my own team would look suspicious and delays a favorable setup for evil.
Player 1: I know player 3 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team includes one evil and one unknown/good-looking player 0. On quest 1 with a 2-person team, as evil I generally want early evil presence on teams while avoiding obvious coordination tells. Approving a team with my teammate on it is strong for evil because it can fail if desired, and my approve vote is not too revealing in round 1 since many players approve opening teams.

As Minion on quest 1 with a 2-player team [0,3], an early fail creates suspicion on me and/or 0, and since I proposed this team it strongly implicates me. Optimal evil play is often to pass the first quest to preserve cover and create uncertainty. Passing reveals less information and helps evil longer-term.
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 3 are evil among visible evil players. The current phase is quest voting on team [0,3], and as a Good player I should always play pass; failing is not available to Good and would only hurt my team if it were. My vote reveals nothing beyond standard Good play, and passing preserves plausible deniability while allowing evil 3 to expose themselves by sabotaging if they choose.

Player 4: Quest 1 team [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and 3 look somewhat good though one evil could have passed. As a Servant, I want to approve strong teams that include prior successful members. Proposed team [0,3,4] keeps the full successful pair and adds me, which is a reasonable, information-preserving good team. Approving helps continue with the best evidence available and does not reveal anything harmful beyond a normal good vote.
Player 0: I am Merlin and know players 1 and 3 are evil. The proposed team [0,3,4] includes a known evil player, so as Good I should reject it. Approving would risk a fail and gives evil control; rejecting signals resistance to teams with suspicious members, though as Merlin I should avoid being too obvious, but this team is clearly bad from my information.
Player 2: Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,3], so 0 and 3 each look somewhat more likely good, though evil can pass early. We have little hard info on 4. As a good Servant in team voting, the best play is usually to approve teams that include previously successful members and avoid unnecessary rejections early. This team expands the successful core [0,3] by adding 4, which is reasonable for quest 2 and may generate useful information. My vote reveals mild trust in the prior success team and willingness to test 4; that generally helps good more than rejecting without evidence.
Player 1: I know player 3 is my evil teammate. The previous team [0,3] succeeded with 0 fails, which is consistent with 3 choosing to hide. This new team [0,3,4] again includes 3 but not me. As evil, I generally want flexibility and to avoid making 3 look too suspicious too early. Approving keeps an evil player on the quest and a 3-person team gives us the option to fail if useful, though 3 may still choose to pass for cover. My vote reveals limited info since evil can plausibly approve a team that includes a prior successful pair.
Player 3: I am Minion with teammate 1. Team [0,3,4] includes me but not my teammate. Last quest [0,3] succeeded with 0 fails; since I was on it, I likely chose pass to avoid outing myself. On a 3-person team for quest 2, having exactly one evil (me) gives evil a chance to fail while preserving ambiguity, though approving a team without player 1 may slightly distance us. As evil, I want teams with at least one evil approved; rejecting could look suspicious after I was on the successful prior team. Approving reveals little and gives me opportunity to fail on quest if needed.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 3 are evil. Team [0,4] excludes known evil players and is the strongest available 2-person team. Previous fail on [0,3,4] is fully explained by 3 being evil; 4 remains plausible good. Approving helps send a likely all-good team and does not reveal extra information beyond normal Merlin-good behavior.
Player 3: I know player 1 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 team [0,3,4] failed with exactly one fail, and I was on it, so from others' perspective either 3 or 4 or possibly 0 is suspicious. Proposed team [0,4] excludes both known evils, so it is likely an all-good team and bad for evil if approved. In team voting as Minion, optimal play is usually to reject clean good teams and approve teams containing evil. Rejecting here helps prevent a probable success and is consistent with suspicion around 4 after the last failed quest. My vote reveals limited info since good players may also reject due to distrust of 4 or the 2-player composition.
Player 4: Quest 1 team [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and 3 look somewhat good. Quest 2 team [0,3,4] failed with exactly one fail; since I am Good, at least one of 0 or 3 is Evil. Team [0,4] excludes 3, so if 0 is Evil this team is guaranteed bad; if 3 is Evil then this team is safe because I am Good. As Good on team voting, I should reject teams with significant evil risk, especially a 2-person team centered on the ambiguous player 0 and me. Rejecting preserves caution and signals suspicion of 0 after the Q2 fail.
Player 2: I’m Good Servant. Q1 team [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and 3 are somewhat good-leaning. Q2 team [0,3,4] had exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 0/3/4 is evil; since 0 and 3 were on the successful Q1 team, 4 is the strongest suspect, though one of 0 or 3 could still be evil who passed Q1. Proposed Q3 team [0,4] includes the most suspicious player and omits the relatively safer 3, so as Good I should reject to avoid sending a likely evil on the quest. Rejecting also signals suspicion on 4 and preference for teams built around Q1 success members.
Player 1: I know player 3 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 team [0,3,4] had exactly one fail, which could have come from either 3 or 4 from others' perspective; since 3 and I know 4 is good, 3 likely supplied the fail and now 4 may look suspicious. The current team [0,4] excludes both evils, so it would certainly succeed and give good a second quest. As evil in the voting phase, optimal play is to reject good-leaning teams that exclude all known evil. Rejecting also keeps ambiguity around 4/3 and avoids helping good lock in a clean pair. My reject signals some resistance but that's acceptable; approving would help good more than it helps my cover.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 3 are evil. The proposed team [0,1] definitely includes an evil player, so as Good I should reject it. Rejecting also avoids endorsing a team that can fail and gives minimal extra info beyond consistent good play.
Player 3: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate. Team [0,1] includes one evil and excludes me. Previous fail on [0,3,4] came from me as Minion, which may make 3 look suspicious, so approving a team with 1 but not me can both advance evil influence and reduce heat on 3. On team voting as evil, optimal play is to approve teams containing evil when plausible. Approving also avoids strongly linking me against teammate 1.
Player 2: I’m Good Servant. Quest 1 team [0,3] succeeded, so at least one of 0/3 is likely good. Quest 2 team [0,3,4] had exactly one fail, so 4 is suspicious or one of 0/3 is evil; since [0,3] already succeeded, 4 is the strongest suspect. A team [0,1] excludes 4 and keeps 0, which is plausible for a 2-player quest. As good, I should approve teams that avoid the main suspect and can generate information. Approving [0,1] is better than rejecting a reasonable team early in the round.
Player 4: I am Good Servant. Quest 1 on [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and 3 look somewhat better. Quest 2 on [0,3,4] failed with exactly one fail; since I am good, at least one of 0 or 3 is evil. Team [0,4] was heavily rejected, suggesting others distrust 0 or me; since I know I am good, 0 becomes more suspicious. Proposed team [0,1] drops both proven-good-me and 3, and includes suspicious 0 plus unknown 1. As Good in team voting, I should reject teams that are likely to contain evil. Rejecting reveals I do not trust 0-centered teams, which is fine and helps good coordinate.
Player 1: I know player 3 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 failed on team [0,3,4], so one of {3,4} could have failed; since 3 is evil, that failure likely came from 3, which makes 4 more likely good from others' perspective. My proposed team [0,1] includes me and excludes known evil teammate 3, which can help me look good. In team voting as evil Assassin, the optimal play is usually to approve teams containing myself, especially on a 2-person quest where I can guarantee a fail if sent. Approving also aligns with being the proposer and avoids suspicious inconsistency. This reveals willingness to go on the mission, but that is beneficial because it preserves cover while giving evil a strong chance to score another fail.

experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I know Player 3 is my evil teammate, and I am evil. The proposed team [1,2,3] contains both evil players (1 and 3), which gives us strong control over quest 4. Since evil already leads 2-1, approving a team with both evil players is optimal because one fail secures the game. Approving does reveal that I support a team including myself and 3, but winning immediately outweighs any informational downside.
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 3 are evil. The proposed team [1,2,3] contains two known evil players, so it is extremely likely to fail and approving gives evil their third quest. In team voting, my optimal play is to reject any team containing known evil, especially at 2-1 evil lead on quest 4. Rejecting is also consistent with good play and does not reveal more than necessary beyond opposing a bad team.
Player 3: I know player 1 is my evil teammate, and I am player 3 evil. The proposed team [1,2,3] contains both evils, so if approved we can guarantee a fail and win immediately since evil already has 2 quest fails. In team voting, optimal evil play is to approve teams that include enough evil to fail, especially a game-winning quest. My approve vote is consistent with wanting this strong evil team through; while it may reveal some preference, securing the immediate win matters most.
Player 2: I am Good and need to avoid any team likely containing Evil because Evil is one fail away from winning after two failed quests. Known info: Quest 1 [0,3,4] had 0 fails, so among 0,3,4 all are likely good except hidden possibilities constrained by later results. Quest 2 [0,1] had 1 fail, so at least one of 0 or 1 is evil. Since 0 was on the successful first quest, 1 is the strongest evil suspect. Therefore team [1,2,3] includes highly suspicious 1. As a Servant, rejecting a risky team is optimal. My vote also signals that I do not trust player 1 based on quest history, which helps Good coordinate.
Player 4: Known info: Quest 1 [0,3,4] succeeded, so likely 0/3/4 are good or at least contained no fail. Quest 2 [0,1] failed, implying at least one of 0 or 1 is evil; combining with Quest 1 makes 1 the strongest evil candidate. Two evil total in 5p. Proposed team [1,2,3] includes 1, who is highly suspicious, and excludes me/4 from the known successful team. As Good servant on quest 4 with evil already at 2 wins, optimal play is to reject suspicious teams and try to force a team around the known success. My vote signals distrust of player 1 and preference for data-consistent teams, which helps Good.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 3 are evil. Proposed team [1,2,4] includes confirmed evil 1, so approving risks immediate loss on quest 4. As good, I should reject any team containing a known evil when possible. My vote reveals some suspicion of 1, but that is worth it to prevent a likely fail.
Player 2: I know I am Good. Quest 2 [0,1] failed, so at least one of 0/1 is evil. Quest 3 [1,2,4] failed with me on it, so at least one of 1/4 is evil. Since 1 appears on both failed quests, player 1 is the strongest evil candidate. Current proposed team [1,2,4] repeats the exact team that already failed, so as Good I should reject it. Rejecting also signals consistency and avoids sending a likely evil team on a must-win quest for Good.
Player 3: I know Player 1 is evil with me. Team [1,2,4] includes my teammate but not me; since evil already leads 2-1 after two failed quests, approving a team with one known evil is strong because Player 1 can fail and potentially end the game. Voting approve also avoids looking inconsistent with prior evil support patterns and helps pass a dangerous team. The information revealed is limited; if it fails, suspicion may hit 1/2/4, but evil only needs one more point now, so immediate win chance outweighs longer-term cover.
Player 1: I know Player 3 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [1,2,4] contains me but not 3. Two failed quests already with me on one successful/failed pattern suggest suspicion may fall on me, but as evil and already up 2-1, approving a team with me on Quest 4 is strong because I can fail and win immediately. Rejecting risks a different team that could exclude evil. My vote reveals some support for a team containing myself, but the immediate win potential outweighs subtlety.
Player 4: As a good Servant, I know only from outcomes: quest 2 [0,1] failed, so at least one of 0/1 is evil. Quest 3 [1,2,4] failed with me on it, and I am good, so at least one of 1/2 is evil. Combining these, player 1 is the strongest evil suspect. The proposed team [1,2,4] repeats the failed quest-3 team exactly, which is very likely to include evil again and risks handing evil the game on quest 4. Optimal good play is to reject teams containing the strongest suspects, especially an already failed exact lineup. My reject signals consistency and helps prevent an almost certainly bad team from going.

Approving does not strongly expose me because evil should generally support teams containing known evil, and if approved this team can end the game for us.

Player 3: I know player 1 is evil with me. Since evil already leads 2-1, the optimal play is usually to approve teams containing an evil player who can fail quest 4 and win immediately. My vote revealing support for this team is acceptable; mixed voting patterns can be justified because I am on the team and want it to go. Approving best helps evil secure the game.
Player 4: I am Good Servant. Known info: team [0,1] failed with exactly one fail, so at least one of 0 or 1 is evil. Teams [1,2,3] and [1,2,4] were both rejected, giving limited info, but I know I am good so [1,2,4] containing me doesn't clear 1 or 2. Current proposed team [0,3,4] includes me and avoids player 1, who is highly suspicious from the only failed approved team. It still includes 0, who is also suspicious, but with quest 4 at 1-2, Good must take some risk; among available options this is a reasonable test of 0 with two others not directly tied to the fail. Voting approve may help find a viable team, while rejecting could push toward random later approvals. Information revealed: approving a team with only one strong suspect is acceptable; if it fails, 0 becomes very likely evil since I know I will pass and 3 is less implicated so far.
